g.data-narration: Welcome to mathematical analysis of recursive algorithms. We will explore techniques to analyze their time and space complexity.
Recursive algorithms call themselves to solve smaller problem instances. Understanding their complexity is crucial for efficient software design.
g.text: Why Do We Need Special Analysis Techniques?

g.data-narration: A recurrence relation defines sequence terms using previous terms. For algorithms, we express running time T(n) as a function of input size n.
g.text: d = exponent of n in f(n)

g.data-narration: The substitution method involves guessing the solution form and proving it correct through mathematical induction.
g.text: Also called "guess and verify" - two key steps:
1. Guess the form of the solution
Assume T(m) ≤ c·m·log m for m < n
Substitute into recurrence, prove for n

g.data-narration: Let us prove T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n is O(n log n) using the substitution method with detailed induction steps.
g.text: Problem: Prove T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n is O(n log n)
= cn·log n - cn + n ≤ cn·log n
Choose c ≥ 1, base case holds → Therefore T(n) = O(n log n) ✓

g.data-narration: The recursion tree method visualizes the cost at each level of recursive calls, helping understand the overall complexity pattern.
g.text: Visualize the cost breakdown across all levels of recursive calls:

g.data-narration: For T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n², each level has decreasing cost. The geometric series converges to O(n²).
The Master Theorem provides direct solutions for recurrences of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). Three cases determine the complexity.
g.text: For T(n) = aT(n/b) + Θ(n^d) where a ≥ 1, b > 1, d ≥ 0:

g.data-narration: Let us apply the Master Theorem to three classic examples: binary search, merge sort, and Strassen's matrix multiplication.
This table shows common recursive algorithms and their time complexities, essential for quick reference during exams.
Space complexity of recursive algorithms depends on maximum recursion depth. The call stack space often differs from time complexity.
g.text: Maximum recursion depth determines stack space. Time and space complexity often differ:

g.data-narration: Let us analyze merge sort step by step using Master Theorem. We identify parameters a=2, b=2, d=1 and apply Case 2.
g.text: Example 1: Merge Sort Analysis using Master Theorem
Problem: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Θ(n) - Find time complexity

g.data-narration: Using recursion tree for T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n², we calculate costs at each level and sum the geometric series.
g.text: Example 2: T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n² using Recursion Tree
At each level i: 3^i nodes × (n/4^i)² = n² × (3/16)^i

g.data-narration: Three methods solve recurrences: substitution for proving bounds, recursion trees for visualization, and Master Theorem for quick divide-and-conquer solutions.
g.text: Master Theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + Θ(n^d) → If a < b^d: Θ(n^d) | a = b^d: Θ(n^d log n) | a > b^d: Θ(n^(log_b a))

g.data-narration: Follow these exam tips: identify recurrence type first, memorize Master Theorem cases, draw recursion trees for visualization, and verify base cases.
